data.title: AB Volvo: NA Truck Lines Are Just About To Scale
data.description:
AB Volvo (VOLAF)(VLVLY) put in a relatively resilient performance in organic terms, ignoring the FX effects from having a significant North American presence and reporting in SEK. While there was some moderation of demand in their end markets, not least because of tariffs where there is plenty of domestic US competition, they are ramping some of their new offerings which we discussed in our previous coverage with production stops now overcome, benefiting from learning economies, while also divesting and discontinuing some less profitable business lines. In terms of mix, there was also a pickup in service as part of revenue, which should be 50%+ higher in margin than the vehicles sales revenue.

Results

On a headline, unadjusted basis, services shrunk slower than vehicles. On an organic basis service revenues grew 6% organically against flat organic growth for the vehicles business. These positive mix effects accrued over both the heavy construction and trucks business apparently, and lead to an overall expansion in organically adjusted EBIT margin, helped also by some discontinued businesses, such as Rokbak which lost too much scale also due to the Russian market falling out, as well as some other divestments that made the business more lean. Adjusted operating income was down less than 10% thanks to the positive mix effects from discontinuations and general lineup revamp, with the CE mix effects seen in the resilient operating income performance for the segment. Negative pressures on the adjusted income were freight costs, which will climb into the coming quarter on account of the Iran War and higher oil prices likely dominating more of Q2 than they did of Q1, as well as tariff costs which would have pressured margins. There was also a positive effect from net R&D on account of the major developments, concentrated it would seem on Mack Trucks, now in the rear view with the commercialisation of these new offerings the current challenge, perhaps dragging for the last time on the current results with blue skies ahead. R&D was down almost 15% in expenses.

The outlook for trucks is quite decent, excepting of course macro pressures that might result from a possibly ongoing Iran War (Germany for example cut growth outlook), and the construction equipment market is also beginning to look up a bit with book to bills above 1x and YoY growth organically of 16%, adjusted for more than just FX. There has also been some positive moves in buses, driven by Prevost, concentrated on North America.

Similar to in our previous coverage, what we are most focused on is the pressures still ongoing on revamps in the Truck offering in the North American market.

For trucks, our European business performance compensated the under-absorption cost from the U.S. truck manufacturing operations that were standing still approximately 25%-30% of the available time in the quarter... we now have available production capacity to meet the increased demand we have seen recently. As from May, we are in balance in North America.

~Martin Lundstedt, CEO of Volvo Group, Q1 Earnings

These model changeovers were becoming a pressure on results as of the last call we covered as well, and Trucks, usually the stalwart business, was the underperformer for the moment. With considerable underabsorption this quarter on a quarter dominated by total production stops, we expect considerable operating income improvement coming from truck vehicle contribution as the production ramps, with associated fixed costs in R&D having also come down.

Bottom Line

Service pick up is already positive, and the adjusted profits have been defensible. This quarter does mark a transition, including discontinuations and new launches, that we expect to prime profitability as industrial scale kicks in. The SEK price of Volvo is 323, with 16 SEK in annualized EPS that's around a 20x estimated annualized P/E, based on these first quarter results where income is being penalized by effects that should mean current profits are a bit of a bottom for the company as Mack scales and other profit measures kick in. Despite these temporary pressures on profits, the PE is still lower than something like PACCAR Inc. (PCAR) at 28x. We continue to prefer Volvo relatively. However, this isn't a particularly pro-cyclical business. Higher capital costs could hit real estate again and cut at the legs a construction equipment recovery. It is a broad based risk due to the systemic importance of oil. Additionally, growth hits could crimp on logistics markets and affect book build for trucks, but a baseline level of scaling with production stops going into the rear view seems inevitable to us and we'd expect some amount of rebound in operating profit regardless.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Rare Chance To Buy Blackstone's $1.3 Trillion Empire At A Deep Discount
data.description:
I last covered Blackstone Inc. (BX) back in August 2025 and rated the stock a Hold. Since then, BX has pulled back sharply, in large part due to negative media narratives around the private credit space, in which BX has significant exposure. In this article, I am going to re-look at the firm in light of its recently released Q1 results and share why I think it is an attractive buy on its recent dip.

My previous Hold rating was based on its stretched valuation multiple, as well as the meaningful macro and geopolitical risks that BX is exposed to, given its global presence, including substantial exposure to China. However, the significant decline in its stock price, despite continued underlying growth, means that it has now moved into value territory in my view.

Q1 Earnings: The Numbers Tell a Different Story

Its Q1 results told that story. For example, distributable earnings came in up 25% year over year and beat consensus estimates in the process, at $1.36 per share. Fee-related earnings also came in 23% higher than the year-ago period. In fact, it was one of the three best fee-related earnings quarters in the company's history, its best non-Q4 result ever. Total management fees came in at a record $2.1 billion and were up 13% year over year. Base management fee growth was up 14% in private equity, 15% in credit, and 21% in Blackstone Multi-Asset Investing (BXMA).

Assets under management were 12% higher than a year ago and came in at a whopping $1.3 trillion, making it the largest alternative asset manager in the world. Over the past 12 months alone, it has raised $250 billion, and that pace remained very strong in Q1 at $69 billion. The company also has substantial net accrued performance revenue of $7 billion, which was up 9% year over year and at its highest level in three and a half years, reflecting $5.69 per share of embedded future earnings power.

Infrastructure and AI: BX's Most Powerful Growth Engine

The company's growth is being driven primarily by its infrastructure business, which grew by a whopping 41% year over year to $84 billion. Its infrastructure business has delivered a 19% net annual return since inception, well above its original 10 to 12% target rate. In Q1 alone, its infrastructure funds appreciated 7.8%, reflecting 25% 12-month appreciation.

Its data center business is seeing particularly strong growth, with a $160 billion data center pipeline making it arguably the world's largest private investor in AI-related infrastructure. As a result, BX is even looking to launch a new public company for its data center business, which should help create a very attractive source of permanent capital for the firm. BX also owns the longest cross-country natural gas pipeline network in the United States, which should profit immensely from natural gas demand fueled by the data center boom in the United States.

Credit Platform: Institutions Are Voting With Their Capital

The company's credit platform, despite being subject to a barrage of negative media headlines, continued to grow at a 15% year-over-year clip, with $40 billion worth of inflows in Q1 alone. In fact, it was one of the best quarters ever for fundraising from institutions and insurance clients. That said, much of the strong growth was seen in areas outside of direct lending, with its investment-grade private credit platform growing 23% year over year. Its insurance business grew by 18% year over year.

The company's direct lending private fund, known as Blackstone Private Credit Fund (BCRED), on the other hand, saw an outflow of $1.4 billion in the first quarter due to retail investors being influenced by negative media press. Institutional demand for the fund remained strong, and most importantly, BCRED's performance remains strong at a 9.4% net annualized rate of return since inception, which is about 60% above the leveraged loan index over the same period of time. BX's opportunistic credit fund also posted strong growth, with over $10 billion of fundraising, and was meaningfully oversubscribed during the quarter. It is also worth noting that BX insiders helped to meet redemption requests at BCRED that were over the 5% gate, thereby effectively doubling down even more than many of BX's peers on private credit, reflecting significant confidence. BX also continues to remain very bullish on the private wealth channel, as assets under management in this segment grew by 14% year over year, including in its private equity, REIT, and additional product pipelines. This brought total private wealth sales to $10 billion in Q1, including $7 billion for perpetual SaaS strategies.

Why the Risk-Reward Has Flipped in BX's Favor

Overall, BX's portfolio continues to generate strong performance across all of its strategies in terms of total returns for investors, and fundraising remains strong everywhere, with the primary sticking point being in its retail-focused BCRED fund. When you combine the overall strength for BX with its attractive valuation due to its significant pullback, attractive dividend yield, and continued strong growth momentum, I think it looks like an attractive buy on the dip.

Of course, no investment is risk-free. Continued macro uncertainty, both in the economic and geopolitical domains, could threaten to weaken deal flow, performance, and some of its more internationally exposed funds, as well as its fundraising. Continued negative press around private credit could weigh on one of its stronger growth engines, even though institutions and other credit-related strategies are driving strong growth in its overall credit business right now. It is this weakness in its retail direct lending business, however, that likely drove the stock lower in the immediate aftermath of it reporting its Q1 results.

It's also worth noting that BX, at $1.3 trillion in assets under management, needs to raise a lot of money to continue driving growth. It is also worth noting that BX does not have all permanent capital, so there is a need to constantly raise capital simply to maintain its current AUM level.

Why I'm Upgrading BX to Strong Buy, and What's Still Holding It Back

While BX is not my highest conviction pick in the alternative asset management space due to the fact that it is a bit more geopolitically exposed than some and is also unlikely to grow as rapidly as some of its smaller peers due to its immense size, and of course its valuation is not quite as compelling as some of its peers, I still think that BX is a very attractive buy on the dip. I'm upgrading it to a strong buy accordingly. As a result, it is on our watch list as we continue to favor other alternative asset managers and run a 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market beta portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: KB Financial: Staying Bullish On Earnings Beat And ROE Expansion Potential
data.description:
I've kept KB Financial Group Inc. (KB) (105560.KS) as a Buy-rated name.

The stock is attractively-valued based on the comparison of its "Return On Equity/ROE" and P/B multiple. The 1Q2026 results were also a favorable surprise.

The firm's generous stockholder returns and street-beating 1Q25 numbers were highlighted in my earlier April 30, 2025 write-up.

1Q2026 Earnings Surpassed Expectations

The group issued a 6-K disclosing its latest financials this Thursday.

KB's Jan-Mar '26 bottom-line went up 12% to ₩1.89T on a year-on-year basis. As per S&P Capital IQ, that was 8% above the analysts' ₩1,753B forecast.

In my opinion, outperformance in non-banking businesses, leaner operations, and stable asset quality provided major tailwinds.

Its non-interest income was 28% higher YoY at ₩1,651B for 1Q26. Also, the non-bank entities' bottom-line contribution grew 1ppt to 43% over the same period. Both figures represented new historic three-month peaks.

I think that the brokerage & wealth management arm "KB Securities" was a stand-out. This unit's "Assets Under Management" or "AUM" grew 56% YoY to ₩240T in the recent quarter.

Separately, KB reported a 1Q2026 "Cost-to-Income Ratio/CIR" of 35.4%. That's 400bps below what it achieved for full-year 2025. Management attributed this to "ongoing efforts to enhance workforce efficiency" at the results meeting.

Its first quarter net profit was also boosted by a 25% YoY reduction in credit impairment charges to ₩493B.

KB's "Non-Performing Assets/NPA" ratio remained comfortable at 0.73% for 1Q26. That's even better than 1Q25's 0.76%. My take is that its healthy loan book allowed for lower provisioning.

Anticipating Financial Return Improvement In FY2026

The company's ROE went from FY24's 9.7% to FY25's 10.9%. My bet is on another 50bps increase to 11.4% this year.

I've identified several levers which are outlined below.

A key driver is "Net Interest Margin" or "NIM" accretion in my view.

At the 4Q25 call in February, management guided for "a gradual decline of NIM for 2026." But KB revised its outlook to a "slight increase" on April 23.

The change in stance is consistent with third-party research organizations' expectations. ING's analysis suggests that "BoK (Bank of Korea) is likely to respond with rate hikes" assuming that "supply constraints" drive up inflation.

I also like how KB is actively lowering its "Cost of Funds/CoF". Its Q1 briefing emphasized "the expansion of core deposits and the rebalancing of the funding portfolio."

A further penetration of overseas markets is another thing you should watch closely.

The firm aims to raise the income share from non-Korean operations from a mid-single-digit percentage last year to high-single-digit levels in FY2026.

According to the Thursday meeting disclosures, its Indonesian affiliate Bukopin's "IT system was upgraded after restructuring for many years." I believe the conclusion of this systems overhaul puts it in a position to capitalize on local opportunities.

"Indonesian banking sector remained stable with a loan growth at 9.6% year-on-year in 2025" as per Bank Rakyat's (BKRKY) Q4 transcript. KB's KRW-denominated advances rose by a more modest 3.8% during the same timeframe. This sends me a clear message regarding the huge potential of emerging economy Bukopin operates in.

I'm also encouraged by the group's strong intent to reshape its portfolio.

Capital Deployment Framework

As per the chart presented above, the bank will be pivoting away from sub-segments that deliver sub-par "Return on Risk-Weighted Assets" or "RoRWA." The funds subsequently freed up will then be re-invested in high-RoRWA verticals. At the 1st quarter meeting, it also stressed that the future focus will be on "high-quality customers."

On the same day as its results release, KB also announced "the cancellation of (14.3M) existing treasury shares" by mid-May.

I estimate that the company's equity base will shrink by almost 4% when the above-mentioned transaction is done. This enhances its future returns by lowering the denominator of the ROE equation.

Shares Are Mispriced

KB is now trading at a trailing price-to-book of 1.04x as per S&P Capital IQ.

I'm valuing this name with the "Gordon Growth Model/GGM." I've assumed an 8% cost of equity (market average), a 3% perpetuity growth (mature economy proxy), and a 11.4% ROE (my prediction).

The target P/B calculated is 1.68 times, implying a +62% (1.68/1.04) upside.

Even if I choose to be conservative, the stock remains meaningfully undervalued. Changing the ROE input to FY25's actual 10.9% will still yield a more demanding P/NAV of 1.58x.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

Prolonged geopolitical instability could hurt the global economy. KB's banking and brokerage businesses are potential victims, being cyclical in nature.

The firm's prospects are also dependent on the success of self-help measures like portfolio restructuring and funding cost optimization. If management executes poorly on these related initiatives, its future ROE could disappoint investors.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm still positive on this stock.

KB's better-than-anticipated 1Q bottom-line is impressive. Its ROE uptrend is sustainable based on my assessment.

data.title: Marvell: Finally Silences The Skeptics, But Don't Rush In Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Marvell Fights Back Against The Skeptics

The AI boom is spreading wildly.

While software folks have been under the weather, the ones building the infrastructure have nothing much to complain about.

And this time, AI networking plays are now on fire!

We already know that memory and storage players continue to be significant beneficiaries, but the focal point has quickly shifted to the networking guys.

The result from this fervor has also seen the stock of Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) break the ceiling of its previous highs with such force that I’m still trying to catch my breath from the surge this week.

In just two months, MRVL has arguably eviscerated the negative sentiments that saw the stock failing to gain traction, as semiconductor stocks continue to print new peaks.

Of course, I’m also concerned with that hockey stick vertical spike in its print, because I don’t think the price action is sustainable without a meaningful retracement.

That said, the fundamental underpinnings for Marvell’s growth thesis through at least 2027 look to be highly secure, following a spate of deals and recent developments.

And for the second fiddle in the networking and the custom XPUs space, the “revenge” has never felt so sweet, especially when panned against Broadcom’s (AVGO) recent performance, outperforming its larger peer since late 2025.

Marvell Brings Nvidia On Board

So, has Marvell finally arrived on the grand stage with the recent victories? Let’s see.

Nvidia's (NVDA) $2B investment opens up a much deeper collaboration than ever before.

I don’t expect anything significant to come out of that in the short term, but the optics of it are good.

Bringing aboard Nvidia’s money, albeit a relatively small sum compared to investments in Intel (INTC), OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO), etc., consolidates Marvell's prowess when contending against Broadcom.

Sure, it’s not as if I think Nvidia and Marvell will be doing a lot of networking or custom chips together for now.

But, I think it allows Nvidia’s partners or customers to now have the chance to work closer with Marvell if they want to go through NVLink Fusion.

Back in my previous MRVL write-up, I had already anticipated that the market was simply too downcast on its prospects.

It just didn’t make a lot of sense to me that the market priced a high-growth company within the optical networking space below its 5-year average.

And, we also haven’t considered the XPU custom opportunity beyond Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT)?

Because that was exactly what happened, right?

Marvell Benefits From The Amazon-Anthropic Deal

I don’t think I need to repeat it, but just a timely reminder on the extension of the partnership between Amazon and Anthropic.

This upside could be massive, as it totally upgrades the visibility of Tranium's future generations. Note that Anthropic has committed to spending $100B with AWS in the next decade.

Thus, if there’s one big growth vector that I didn’t previously account credibly for, it’s clearly Anthropic.

The AI company has caught up with OpenAI, printing $30 billion in annualized recurring revenue recently.

It has also forced OpenAI to ramp up releasing its model improvements more quickly, as it looks to make up for lost ground in the enterprise space with Claude Code.

Given that Anthropic is arguably short of capacity right now as it deals with the growth inflection spurred by the explosion of Claude Code workloads, this has also benefited Amazon, which most certainly extends to Marvell.

Since we know that because Marvell and Amazon have a well-established relationship with its Trainium generations, we now have line of sight visibility over the next few years, not just in terms of XPUs, but also across the entire networking stack.

Plus, we haven’t even considered the potential of Amazon selling its custom AI chips to third-party customers.

Therefore, I think investors have suddenly awakened to the fact that maybe the 50% growth in the optical portfolio over the past five years might even be surpassed.

Revised estimates from Wall Street indicate that the advanced optical portfolio could double in 2026 and then in 2027 too!

So, all these recent developments arguably will help drive further upside into the forecasts. I believe the market has clearly raised the bar for management to cross when it next reports its earnings on April 28th.

And then, we still haven’t accounted for the co-packaged optics opportunity from 2027, so there’s really a lot going on right now, I think.

It seems like underlying demand is so voracious that companies are desperately looking to find as much compute and networking opportunities as they can right now.

And they are spreading the coverage to as many suppliers and designers as possible.

Marvell & The New Google Surprise Report!

And then, the Google (GOOGL, GOOG) deal!

That one took me by surprise. Because Google has been working with Broadcom for the past seven generations.

We all know that Broadcom charges high margins, right? Broadcom reports overall gross margins of >75%. Yet, it’s also best-in-class, right?

Google’s shifting some work to MediaTek already shows us that the search leader could be keen to find some opportunities to diversify the relationship a bit.

So, I clearly didn’t think that Google could be working with Marvell on two chips, right?

One is a memory processor alongside its famed TPU, while the other is a new custom AI chip specially designed for Google.

I think this clearly validates the technological superiority of Marvell’s stack. This one is likely based on its Structera CXL solutions.

It does seem like the hyperscaler discussions are now coming to fruition for Marvell, and it’s really bringing the “A” game to Broadcom to win over these big tech customers.

For MRVL, it doesn’t need to become the primary designer for Google; it just needs to win some first, and then give Google a second look for future generation needs.

I don’t think Broadcom is going to lower its margins just because of these new imperatives. In contrast, Marvell might see better economics and scale, as it expands into the Google business.

MRVL: Earnings Moving In The Right Direction

Nevertheless, I don’t expect a near-term growth inflection for the current calendar year, but the market looks ahead, right?

Marvell’s forward earnings outlook is already capturing these newer dynamics, and I could see why.

The market is now finally pricing the company as a faster grower than peers.

Not only are the estimates moving in the right direction, but they are also moving up very quickly.

Given the smaller market share as compared to AVGO, the conviction does make sense. Although the question about timing, execution of these projects, was somewhat more questionable back then, before the recent slew of deals.

And, with Amazon expected to ramp up, plus Google now potentially also being part of its customer slate, the roadmap for the next few years looks immensely secure.

Based on the forecasted revenue growth rate through FY2029, we are looking at >30% growth for the current FY, and then another +30% growth for the next FY.

These are rock-solid prognoses, and thus, it didn’t quite make sense that the stock was priced below 25x forward earnings back then.

Yet, Could All These Have Already Been Priced In?

However, even as MRVL appears to have broken out resoundingly against its previous peak, notched in early 2025, the print also looks suspiciously vertical.

If I were to be forthright about the price chart, we mus'nt throw caution to the wind right now.

I would err on the side of caution to see whether we could see a pullback to the January 2025 level first, at the $130 zone.

We can take things from there, and who knows, it might even break down below it, giving us a better opportunity to get in at a lower price point.

Moreover, MRVL is now valued at >43x forward earnings, so well off those lows in March 2026.

And note that MRVL has had trouble getting past the 50x earnings level over the past five years.

Clearly, the market has marked up the optimism so quickly, investors have rushed into the stock too quickly and too fervently, and that’s undisputedly shown in the price action.

I’m not sounding the clarion call for you to cut, because I think MRVL continues to look well-positioned as a buy-and-hold.

Yet, rushing in now when a vertical spike has been printed, and with multiples now flashing caution, doesn’t seem too wise, in my opinion.

Therefore, I think it’s timely for me to step back and reconsider how this breakout will unfold, and assess where the following consolidation zone could be after the optimism cools down.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: IPO Activity Dipped In Q1, But Don't Call It A Downturn
data.description:
By Phil Mackintosh, Nasdaq Chief Economist, and Michael Normyle, U.S. Economist and Senior Director at Nasdaq

In the first quarter, there seemed to be one thing after another to create further uncertainty for markets.

  • The Artificial Intelligence (AI)-related selloff that began late last year spilled into early 2026 as investors worried about spending on AI and the businesses it could disrupt.
  • We also saw a(nother) partial government shutdown, impacting the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, which needs to review initial public offering (IPO) filings.
  • Then came the Supreme Court decision to undo the International Emergency Economic Powers Act tariffs, upending trade policy.

And after all that, the Iran conflict has roiled markets.

Iran conflict boosts energy commodities, but hurts bonds, stocks and gold

From a macro and markets perspective, the biggest impact of the conflict has been on energy, which has rallied significantly from the start of the conflict through the end of March (the relevant period for our IPO Pulses). European natural gas prices were up about 60%, while U.S. and global oil prices were up 40-50% (black bars).

That’s because Iran effectively stopped traffic in the Strait of Hormuz - typically the route for 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies. The conflict has also damaged energy infrastructure across the Persian Gulf region, not just in Iran.

Chart 1: Attacks and Strait of Hormuz closure squeezing commodity supplies, increasing prices

But it wasn’t just energy commodities that saw prices rise. The Persian Gulf region is also a top exporter, often via the Strait of Hormuz, for other key commodities, including:

  • Urea, a fertilizer produced using natural gas, has surged about +35%. The region also makes half the global supply.
  • Aluminum has jumped around +10%, as the region exports a tenth of global supply.
  • Helium, which is an input in semiconductor manufacturing, is not exchange-traded but Qatar exports a third of global supply, so prices have increased.

The higher commodity prices have already boosted the price of gas at the pump, which affects inflation. U.S. headline CPI increased to 3.3% per annum in March, up from 2.4% in February. As a result, U.S. bond yields increased across the yield curve, with the fear that the Federal Reserve will need to raise rates to tackle inflation before it starts a wage-price spiral. U.S. 10-year Treasury yields are up 35 basis points from the start of the conflict through the end of March, meaning bond values fell.

Those higher yields hurt gold prices (-10%), too, making gold relatively less attractive, since it doesn’t pay interest.

While bonds and gold haven’t played their usual role as safe haven assets, the U.S. dollar has (+2%) to a degree. That’s been an added headwind to foreign equities (in dollar terms), which were down 10% or more, while U.S. equity indexes were down just 5% - although this difference also reflects that many foreign companies and economies are much more reliant on energy from the Strait of Hormuz.

Stocks not only sold off but also were more volatile, moving with each new headline, which boosted the VIX by over 25% (red bar).

Still, between the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on April 8 and talks of a peace deal, a lot of these prices have reverted somewhat (circles), including U.S. equities recently rising to record highs.

Iran conflict knocks IPO Pulses from highs to lows… for now

The uncertainty caused by the Iran conflict (and the other disruptions this year) has meant that IPO activity got off to a slow start this year in the U.S. and Stockholm.

Nasdaq U.S. IPO Pulse

Coming into 2026, the first quarter seemed primed for a rebound, as the record 43-day government shutdown in the fourth quarter of last year could have pushed some IPOs into the new year. But that hasn’t been the case for operating companies, with 26 IPOs in the first quarter - a 35% drop from the quarter prior.

Still, overall IPO activity (including special purpose acquisition companies) actually held up, with the first quarter seeing two more IPOs (88) than the fourth quarter (86) - and both quarters raised about $21 billion.

With the Iran conflict driving a selloff in stocks and increasing volatility, it’s weighed on the Nasdaq IPO Pulse, which fell to a nine-month low in March.

But, as we’ve noted before, for the IPO Pulse to truly signal a turn in IPO activity, its change in direction needs to last at least a few months. So far, the IPO Pulse is just two months removed from January’s 15-month high, meaning it’s too soon to suggest the broader uptrend in U.S. IPO activity is over, especially with U.S. equities recovering to record highs and volatility easing in April.

Chart 2: Nasdaq IPO Pulse drops to 9-month low, but too soon to call downturn

Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse

Like in the U.S., Nasdaq Stockholm saw fewer new listings in the first quarter (3) than the fourth quarter of last year (5). Of course, in capital-raised terms, there’s no way to compete with the fourth quarter, when Stockholm had Europe’s biggest IPO in three years - Verisure (VSURE).

And similar to its U.S. counterpart, the Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse fell in March, dropping to a seven-month low. Here, too, since it’s just a one-month drop, it’s premature to call an end to the upturn in Stockholm IPO activity, especially with the IPO Pulse reaching a 20-month high in February. Plus, also like in the U.S., European stocks are nearing record highs in April.

Chart 3: Nasdaq Stockholm IPO Pulse dips to seven-month low from 20-month high

IPO Pulses not yet in downturns, so the first quarter may prove to be a blip

Given the ongoing Iran conflict and other disruptions in the first quarter, it’s no wonder IPO activity got off to a slow start in 2026.

Still, since the dips in the IPO Pulses fall well short of signaling a downturn in IPO activity and U.S. and European equities have rebounded in April, it’s entirely possible that the first quarter’s softer activity will prove to be a blip. We’ll see when we have our next update in July.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon Q1 Preview: Watch The Capex And EBIT Margins Print
data.description:
Performance assessment

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has lagged the broader market index slightly since my last update on the stock.

So I think my Hold view has been appropriate so far.

Elevator pitch

I am getting bullish on Amazon ahead of its earnings release on 29 April '26:

  1. There are 3 key reasons for why I'm not alarmed by high capex spend.
  2. AWS is losing market share but this matters less as absolute growth is strong.
  3. In-house AI chips can improve long term margins.
  4. There is some valuation risk.
  5. AMZN is breaking out and looks ready to make more new highs.

There are 3 key reasons for why I'm not alarmed by high capex spend

One figure many investors will be watching in the upcoming Q1 FY26 release would be capex, especially after the market took the initial news of Amazon's $200B capex spend (vs $150B consensus) negatively.

But I am not as concerned by this high capex spend for a few reasons:

Firstly, the spending is rather broad-based and not merely focused on AI infrastructure:

With such strong demand for our existing offerings and seminal opportunities like AI, chips, robotics, and low earth orbit satellites, we expect to invest about $200 billion in capital expenditures across Amazon in 2026, and anticipate strong long-term return on invested capital.

- CEO Andy Jassy in the Q4 FY25 Press Release.

This makes direct comparisons to Alphabet's (GOOGL, GOOG) $180B capex guidance for FY26 a bit flawed since Alphabet's spend is largely on AI infrastructure in data centers.

Secondly, there is a good amount of visibility and customer lock-in for a bulk of the expected capex spend:

Of the AWS capex we expect to spend in 2026, much of which will be monetized in 2027-2028, we already have customer commitments for a substantial portion of it.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

And thirdly, betting big to seize a dominating position in future markets is not uncharacteristic of Amazon. In fact, it is perfectly in-line with its investment philosophy that Founder Jeff Bezos outlined in 1997, and which is still applicable today (the annual shareholder letters always attach the original 1997 letter to affirm that the same principles still apply each year):

We will continue to make investment decisions in light of long-term market leadership considerations rather than short-term profitability considerations or short-term Wall Street reactions...

We will make bold rather than timid investment decisions where we see a sufficient probability of gaining market leadership advantages. Some of these investments will pay off, others will not, and we will have learned another valuable lesson in either case...

We will balance our focus on growth with emphasis on long-term profitability and capital management. At this stage, we choose to prioritize growth because we believe that scale is central to achieving the potential of our business model.

- Excerpt's from Amazon's investment philosophy outlined in Founder Jeff Bezos' 1997 Letter to Shareholders.

From a numbers perspective, I expect to see about $45B in capex spend in the Q1 release, corresponding to a TTM capex ramp of $149B. I also want to highlight that $200B TTM capex in Q4 FY26 will lead to a more sustained growth ramp if you look at the growth rates trajectory. On the other hand, prior consensus expectations of $150B expected a sharp drop in TTM capex to 17% YoY:

AWS is losing market share, but this matters less as absolute growth is strong

As part of my coverage of all the major cloud vendors (Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet and even Alibaba (BABA)), I track cloud market shares every quarter. And over the past few quarters, I have noticed that Amazon's AWS has been seeing a bit of a decline, losing share to Microsoft's Azure and especially Google Cloud:

However, I think it would be a blunder to place too much weight on this and view this as a headwind because the absolute growth in AWS and its remaining performance obligations [RPOs, which are a leading indicator of future revenues as they represent contracted work yet to be executed and recognized as revenues) is still very strong:

CC refers to constant currency; it takes out the effects of FX. This is useful to assess underlying performance of the business without counting macro factors.

Here are the key drivers of AWS adoption, summarized by the CEO:

First, we have broader capabilities than others, with compelling offerings for model-building... high-performance inference... lower-cost inference (on our custom silicon, Trainium), agent-building... scalable and secure agent environments... turnkey agents for coding, software migrations, and most tasks knowledge workers use...

Second, as customers expand their use of AI, they want their inference to reside near their other applications and data... and much more of it resides in AWS than anywhere else.

Third, as customers expand their AI usage, they consume a lot of additional non-AI services, where AWS also has the broadest and most capable offerings.

And fourth, AWS has the strongest security and operational performance of any AI and infrastructure provider.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

In-house AI chips can improve long term margins

I've recently talked more about hyperscalers investing in their own chips in my recent coverage of Nvidia (NVDA). But for Amazon, I first discussed this strategic move early last year. The big bet Amazon is making is that the market will evolve from prioritizing performance-first to valuing good price to performance metrics. And commentary suggests strong demand and order confirmations to support this:

customers want better price-performance. We’ve seen this movie before. In the CPU space, virtually all of the workloads ran on Intel chips until we invented Graviton in 2018. Graviton, which has up to 40% better price-performance than other x86 processors, is now used expansively by 98% of the top 1,000 EC2 customers...

The same story arc is unfolding in AI. Our second version of our custom AI silicon (Trainium2) had about 30% better price-performance than comparable GPUs, and has largely sold out. Trainium3, which just started shipping at the start of 2026 and is 30-40% more price-performant than Trainium2, is nearly fully-subscribed. A significant chunk of Trainium4, which is still about 18 months from broad availability, has already been reserved.

- CEO Andy Jassy in his FY25 shareholder letter.

EC2 refers to Elastic Compute Cloud, which is Amazon AWS' virtual server service.

According to Jassy, transitioning more to in-house chips can lead to "several hundreds of basis points" improvement in margins. I believe this means we can see mid-high teens EBIT margins in the medium to long term:

But over the short term, I think AI investments and the depreciation costs from that capex may limit some of the margin expansion. Q1 FY26's margin print will give us more clues on the extent of this headwind.

There is some valuation risk

The last time I covered AMZN, it was trading at a 1-yr fwd P/E of <20x. But now, it is priced at a 32.8x. This represents a 50.4% premium to the 21.8x median of its comps:

This 50.4% premium has swung AMZN's relative valuation from the undervalued territory to the overvalued zone, above the typical premium over comps of 27.9%:

Hence, I believe we have to accept that there is some degree of high valuation risk to AMZN stock right now.

AMZN is breaking out and looks ready to make more new highs

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

AMZN Technical Analysis

AMZN stock is printing a big bullish engulf that is breaking monthly resistances, supported by a false breakout or Wyckoff-spring-like initiation. I believe this is a bullish chart setup.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to strong growth in AWS and scope for meaningful margin improvement from in-house AI chips.
  • Valuation: Bearish as AMZN stock is trading more expensively vs peers than usual.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish as the stock shows a compelling breakout setup.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.

data.title: Quest Diagnostics: Good Q1, Solid Business, Steady Share Price Upside Likely
data.description:
Investment Overview

Quest Diagnostics (DGX) announced its Q1 2026 earnings earlier this week. Before we take a detailed look at the numbers, let's remind ourselves of the New Jersey-based Life Sciences core business activities - as per its Q1 2026 quarterly report/10Q filing:

Our diagnostic information services ("DIS") business provides diagnostic insights from the results of our laboratory testing to empower people, physicians, and organizations to take action to improve health outcomes.

Derived from one of the world's largest databases of de-identifiable clinical lab results, our diagnostic insights reveal new avenues to identify and treat disease, inspire healthy behaviors and improve healthcare management.

We provide services to a broad range of customers within our primary customer channels - physicians (including those associated with accountable care organizations and Federally Qualified Health Centers), hospitals, and patients and consumers.

Our other customers include health plans, employers, emerging retail healthcare providers, government agencies, pharmaceutical companies and other commercial clinical laboratories.

Quest reported revenues of $2.9 billion in Q1, up 9.2% year-on-year, and diluted earnings per share of $2.24, up 15.5% year-on-year. Operating income was $399 million - margin of ~14% - cash provided by operations was $278 million, down 12% year-on-year, cash and equivalents were reported as $393 million, total current assets were $2.57 billion, current liabilities were $2.2 billion, and long-term debt was $5.2 billion.

After results were reported, Quest stock dropped in value from ~$208 per share, to ~$199.5, which is the current traded price (at the time of writing), reflecting a market cap valuation of $22.1 billion. Stock is up ~15% year-to-date, >50% on a five-year basis, and >160% on a 10-year basis.

Guidance for 2026 has been provided by the company as follows:

Analysis - Good Growth, Strong Business, But Debt A Slight Concern

When I last covered Quest in a note for Seeking Alpha last October, I was covering Q3 earnings, and I issued stock with a "Buy" rating - shares have risen by just over 10% since.

I wrote that:

If we take a step back and think about how Quest has performed long-term, we may conclude that, after a surge in revenues thanks to the era of COVID testing in 2021 and 2022, growth had been somewhat stagnant until this year.

As we can see above, Quest earned ~$10.8 billion in 2021, thanks to additional COVID testing income, and then experienced a couple of "trough" years when revenues fell year-on-year, and in fact, operating income in 2023 was >$1 billion less than in 2021.

As I predicted in my last note (with the benefit of management's guidance), 2025 was the first year in which Quest earned more revenue than in 2021, although it was not nearly so profitable in 2025 as it was in 2021. Nevertheless, Quest stock trades at a higher value today than it did in December 2021, when it briefly spiked to ~$173 per share.

Based on 2026 guidance, Quest's forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio is <2x, and its forward price-to-earnings ("P/E") ratio ~19x. The latter is a respectable enough figure, although given the P/S ratio is so much lower, we could conclude that lower profitability may be weighing the share price down slightly.

Quest's interest expense in Q1 amounted to ~$67 million, lowering operating income, but management appears to be unconcerned by its debt position - at least there was no mention of it in its Q1 2026 earnings conference call. In its 2025 investor day presentation, however, management targets 4%-5% long-term revenue growth, and 7%-9% adjusted EPS growth, so it seems that improving profitability is a goal of the business.

In that same presentation, Quest shares the following, sobering analysis of a US population that is growing "older and sicker".

It seems hard to deny that an aging U.S. population will likely increase the size of the market that Quest is operating within, which is already vast, as we can see in the slide below:

I made the point in my last note also that Quest's business was similar in size and performance to Labcorp Holdings (LH). Labcorp's current market cap valuation is $21.8 billion, almost the same as Quest's, and its revenues in 2025 amounted to $13.95 billion, with operating income of $1.5 billion, margin of ~11%. Lab Corp also carries ~$5 billion in debt on its balance sheet. Its stock is up 19% on a 1-year basis, 16% on a five-year basis, and >145% on a 10-year basis.

This suggests to me, and again, I made this point in my last post, that the diagnostics industry is characterized by reasonably high barriers to entry, is not wildly profitable, and is a growing industry that offers incumbents the chance to grow their businesses at a steady, but not spectacular, rate.

Improving Health Insurance Industry and PAMA Data Submission Provide Valuable Tailwinds To Quest's Business

In my last note I wrote that:

The key question is whether Quest, or for that matter, LabCorp, has the ability to break new ground in terms of revenue generation. In Quest's case, the new initiatives the company has initiated certainly seem to add obvious value - 200k new patients to test re the Fresenius deal, and the biggest ever revenue opportunity for Co-Lab thanks to the Corewell Health deal.

I noted that large health insurers such as UnitedHealth (UNH), CVS Health (CVS), Elevance Health (ELV) and Humana (HUM) had been suffering, as healthcare utilization was on the rise, while government funding for, e.g. Medicare Advantage healthcare plans was not keeping pace with the increase in utilization, and a migration from fee for service ("FFS") to Value Based Care ("VBC") was not necessarily helping solve this problem, or at least not yet.

Quest notes in its 2025 annual report/10K filing that:

Most of the services we perform are paid for by third-party payers, including large national health plans, regional and local health plans and government payers, which includes Medicare and Medicaid. Through these payers, we estimate that we have access to over 90% of insured lives in the United States. We work with payers to reduce the cost of care, improve the customer experience and drive better outcomes for patients.

In April, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services ("CMS"), which had, earlier in the year, suggested it would hike Medicare Advantage payment rates by just 0.05% in 2027, relented and announced a 2.48% hike instead.

Quest also notes in its 2025 10K filing:

Pursuant to The Protecting Access to Medicare Act of 2014 ("PAMA"), reimbursement rates for many clinical laboratory tests provided under Medicare were reduced during 2018 - 2020. Starting in 2020, Congress has repeatedly acted to delay PAMA implementation by delaying the next round of data reporting (2020-2026) and Medicare cuts (2021-2026). Congress introduced legislation in 2025, the Results Act, which would reform PAMA and create a true market-based CLFS.

On this week's earnings call, Quest's CEO James Davis told analysts:

If you think about it this way, the last time there was a data submission, there were probably 2 companies that submitted over 80% of the data. And so the 2 companies probably -- and we're one of them and our nearest competitor is the second one, we probably have at best 17% to 20% share of the Medicare market, right? We were disproportionately lower in that portion of our business than in other segments because it's any willing provider.

So when only 2 providers submit -- basically 2 providers submit 80% of the data and you have less than 20% of the market, it's obviously going to lead to a very skewed data set. So we're hopeful that the other 80% submit. We know that, that other 80% is paid 2 to 3x Medicare rates by most health plans. And you put all that together, Jack, and it should indicate a price increase.

To summarize, the health insurance industry endured a torrid 2023 and 2024, recovered slightly in 2025, and appears to be back on track in 2026, which is good news for businesses like Quest's, while Quest may finally benefit from a PAMA data submission that forces CMS to increase key diagnostic test payment rates.

As we can see above, Quest is no stranger to inorganic growth; however, I don't foresee much M&A happening in 2026, as, announcing Q4 2025 earnings, Quest raised its quarterly dividend by 7.5% to $0.86 (annual yield of ~1.72%) and announced a $1 billion share buyback program.

Analysis - What's The Play On Quest Today

In terms of my rating on Quest stock, it is very much "as you were." Quest has a strong business, with ~55k employees, focused on core fields of healthcare - oncology, cardiometabolic health, women's and reproductive health, brain health, and autoimmune disorders.

These target markets are only likely to grow thanks to an aging population, and the fact there is broad acknowledgement of this fact means that the CMS is likely to "play ball" in terms of increasing payment rates for the services Quest provides, for itself or on behalf of insurers, which may also aid Quest's profitability; hence, long-term guidance shows greater growth for the bottom line than the top.

These are all positives for Quest, but at the same time we may acknowledge that its industry may never be a high-margin industry, as, for example, the pharmaceuticals industry is, driving operating margins in the >20% range, where diagnostics drives margins closer to 10%-15%.

As such, while I am assigning Quest my second successive "Buy" rating, as I did in my last note, I'd suggest share price growth will be steady but not spectacular - Quest stock is up ~50% on a five-year basis, and my take is that, in another five years' time, it may well have risen by another 50%.

data.title: Intel's AI Comeback
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel’s (INTC) outlook is no longer overlooked but still not fully understood. It’s not a matter of turning things around, it’s a matter of a fundamental shift in where value creation lies in the AI domain. The CPUs have quietly become indispensable once again and Q1 results showed that the demand is not only recovering but is actually limited. It means that the company is still earning below its true worth. In my last coverage I emphasized Intel's CPU comeback that nobody sees and in less than a month the stock has rallied 53%.

EPS Inflection Is Here But the Real Upside Hasn’t Even Started

Not only did EPS exceed expectations but it demonstrated that operating leverage was back sooner than expected. With $0.29 non-GAAP EPS, Intel essentially shortened a multi-year recovery to a number of quarters on the strength of a mix shift towards data center and pricing power. My takeaway is that EPS was still supply-driven, not demand-based. This has significance since it implies that potential for upside to EPS has not been reached yet. The risk is in quality since GAAP loss and high CapEx make this EPS rebound incomplete.

As for the Data Center and Artificial Intelligence group, it is now becoming quite exciting to me. The group managed to grow revenues up by 22% which led to an increase in operating margin of 31%. Indeed, even in competition with AMD (AMD), Intel was able to show fairly impressive results regarding revenue growth rate and this was due to the continuous growth of the addressable market.

Moreover, this is related to the shift in demand pattern. With training taking place using GPU chips, more tasks require the usage of CPUs. These tasks include such things as model management, pipeline control and real-time decision-making process. This, I believe, is the moment where the shift occurs, yet the market has not fully realized it.

As far as the Client Computing Group there was a decrease in revenues to $7.7 billion. First of all, this is associated with increasing penetration of AI-enabled PCs, which implies the fact that now this sector has pricing power missing before. Despite decreasing volumes of PC production forecast, AI-driven upgrades of PCs can offset it.

Profitability Is Back & Cash Flow Still Tells the Real Story

Clearly, profitability inflects, yet it is not clean just yet and the difference between these two statements is important. From a non-GAAP perspective, Intel is showing real operating leverage with its 41% gross margins and increasing operating margins, implying a better mix because of the success of data centers and pricing muscle. This appears to be the first time in years where Intel shows signs of being able to grow profits.

On the GAAP side, however, the numbers paint quite a different picture. A net loss of almost $3.7 billion reflects the costs of rebuilding the company's portfolio and infrastructure. This is not noise, this is the cost of ramping up the foundries and nodes, making the necessary investments. Investors who pay attention only to the bottom line adjusted for certain expenses miss this very important detail of capital intensity involved in this turn.

The point gets even clearer when looking at cash flows. With $2 billion spent on adjusted free cash flows, Intel invested almost $5 billion in CapEx, thus putting pressure on its balance sheet. I would not consider this development a warning light for the company rather an expected consequence of its strategy of building capacity and meeting demand in parallel.

Yet, this situation may get tricky soon unless capex expenditures start to decline or the foundry business starts to generate some outside revenue. In conclusion, profitability may surprise to the upside but financial strength is another story.

Intel Foundry: Expensive Experiment or Hidden Goldmine?

The problem still lies in the foundry segment. Intel earned $5.4 billion in revenue, out of which $174 million came from external sources and its operating loss amounted to $2.4 billion. It is true that there is some improvement in terms of yield but Intel is still an unproven foundry firm.

However, the 18A node is ahead of schedule, the 14A has even more promise out of the gate and advanced packaging may soon be a multibillion-dollar endeavor. That’s where the action lies. The moment that Intel proves that its offerings have an external customer base, everything falls into place. With the extended contracts with Google and even potential ones with Terafab, it is evident that Intel is not a zero-sum game here.

INTC At $80: Overvalued or Early Re-rating?

While trading at around $80, the stock appears pricey at around 64x FY2026 forward P/E and 6x forward P/S. However, this does not consider the earnings inflection currently taking place. Earnings are expected to increase from $1.05 in FY2026 to $1.15 in FY2027 and around $1.76 in FY2028, indicating that the multiple will quickly shrink as earnings stabilize.

If I assume a $1.76 EPS for FY2028 and assign a 40-45x multiple based on the return to AI infrastructure growth and margin improvement, the intrinsic value indicates a range of around $70–$80. Notably, this range does not factor in any upside potential from its foundry business and advanced packaging capabilities.

What makes this analysis interesting is that Intel is no longer a shrinking CPU business, it is a mixed AI infrastructure company. With this story gaining traction, multiple expansion will likely persist alongside earnings growth. Thus, trading at around $80 now seems to be only the beginning of a re-rating process.

The Comeback Isn’t Guaranteed Though

As for the immediate future of Intel, it appears that the main risk here can be defined as lack of competitive performance. Apart from continuing losing market share in server CPUs to AMD, Intel faces problems associated with ARM-based (ARM) solutions offered by hyperscalers. Should the situation remain unchanged, Intel won't be able to capitalize on any upside opportunities.

Additionally, executing the above-mentioned strategy in terms of manufacturing also poses a serious challenge in view of ongoing losses. Absent external demand, the investment case loses some attractiveness. The other risk factor to be considered here is negative cash flows, along with heavy CapEx. Intel would have to come up with some financial options to keep its ability to manufacture parts intact.

Takeaway

Intel is trying to establish its position within the world of AI infrastructure. I believe that the company has successfully accomplished its goals, at least when it comes to generating data center revenues and improving execution. Much work still needs to be done but the trends in demand are moving in the right direction.

data.title: Q1 Earning Preview: Is Alphabet Overspending? A Painful Lesson From Meta And Intel
data.description:
On April 7th, BNP Paribas equity analyst Nick Jones published a note on Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG/GOOL) arguing that investor worries about the company’s elevated AI CapEx spending are overdone. This note piqued our interest because in our internal note, we have previously flagged multiple sell-side analysts’ probes during Alphabet’s Q4 2025 Q&A on potential overspending. It also caught our attention because on the publish date of this note, Google was trading at roughly $300 per share after a 97% YoY return. At that valuation, we didn’t think investors were particularly concerned about Alphabet potentially overspending.

In this article, we will discuss two recent case studies of overspending that we have covered: Meta Platforms (META) in 2022 and Intel (INTC) in 2023. Both companies destroyed hundreds of billions of dollars in investor capital. We draw lessons from these examples, highlight the clear parallels to Google today, and outline the key metrics we will use to monitor Alphabet's earnings on April 29th, 2026.

BNP Paribas’ Note on Google

On April 7th, 2026, equity analyst Nick Jones released a note covering both Alphabet and Amazon (AMZN). He argued that investor worries about their elevated AI/CapEx spending are overdone. His key arguments made on Alphabet:

  • Backlog-to-capex ratio is the right metric, not just spending total. Contracted demand for Google’s cloud/AI infrastructure has grown and aligns with CapEx levels. This suggests Alphabet is not overspending but investing appropriately to fulfil existing demand and avoid capacity shortages.
  • Improving efficiency via revenue per employee: Alphabet is becoming more efficient as revenue per employee rises, countering fears of wasteful AI investments.
  • Custom AI chips provide a structural advantage: Alphabet has a multi-year lead with its Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), developed over a decade and deeply integrated with its hardware and software ecosystem. This lowers total cost of ownership compared to other hyperscalers reliant on third-party GPUs.

Our notes from Alphabet’s Q4 2025 analyst Q&A

One exercise we like to do during earnings is to analyze what sell-side analysts are really asking. Given that analysts must remain polite to maintain relationships with company's management, we try to read between the lines: what exactly are they probing? What are they excited or uneasy about in the company's current trajectory?

In our analysis for Alphabet's Q4 2025 Q&A, we found that some analysts shared uneasiness about the company's CapEx spend. They are particularly interested in how Google plans to bridge the gap between their current spend and future demand.

For example:

Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs

“We've talked a lot about imbalances between demand and capacity for AI, both internally and externally. With the step function change in absolute capital dollars you're projecting now in '26, can you talk about the pathway to closing the gaps or the need for compute, both internally and externally and how to think about some of the outputs of closing that gap as the year progresses? And… at the level of spend that you're now projecting for '26, how do you think about continuing to find operating efficiencies inside the business to fund those investment and growth investments as well?”

Mark Shmulik, Bernstein

“Can you talk a little bit more about the relationship between investment levels and how you kind of expect core performance to trend? Is there like an operating income or a free cash flow objective that you solve towards? Or how do you think about greenlighting resources and projects?”

This shared concern from sell-side analysts about Google’s potential overspending leads us to revisit two case studies, Meta in 2022 and Intel in 2023, where similar “overspend now, demand will come later” narratives played out, ultimately destroying hundreds of billions in investor capital.

Two Case Studies: Meta 2022 and Intel 2023

Meta: The Metaverse Bet

After the 2021 rebrand from Facebook to Meta, Mark Zuckerberg positioned the metaverse as the “successor to the mobile internet.” Just like AI today, his vision was grand. Horizon Worlds was pitched as more than an AR/VR social space, it was to become the fully immersive computing platform where people would live inside the digital world instead of using computers while staring at flat screens. He repeatedly called the project Meta's top priority, promising that Horizon Worlds, Quest headsets, and custom silicon together would create a new ecosystem with powerful network effects and drive long-term revenue.

During the peak of the metaverse push, Zuckerberg called for massive, front-loaded CapEx to build infrastructure and stay ahead of competition.

In the Q1 2022 earnings call:

“In Reality Labs, we're making large investments to deliver the next platform that I believe will be incredibly important both for our mission and business, comparable in value to the leading mobile platforms today. I recognize that it's expensive to build this — it's something that's never been built before...”

In the Q3 2022 earnings call:

“…we’re choosing to keep investing heavily in these areas [AI discovery, ads/messaging, and metaverse].”

“We expect Reality Labs expenses will increase meaningfully again in 2023… Beyond 2023, we expect to pace Reality Labs investments such that we can achieve our goal of growing overall company operating income.”

He also openly accepted potential massive losses in exchange for future leadership in the AR/VR market:

“We do anticipate that Reality Labs operating losses in 2023 will grow significantly year-over-year.”

“There’s still a long road ahead to build the next computing platform, but we are clearly doing leading work here. This is a massive undertaking … But I think our work is going to be of historic importance and create the foundation for an entirely new way that we will interact with each other … And I think it’s some of the most historic work that we’re doing that I think people are going to look back on decades from now and talk about the importance of the work that was done here.”

The result was that Reality Labs' operating losses ballooned from $10.2bn in 2021 to $19.2bn in 2025, while revenue stayed flat at $2.2bn. In March 2026, Meta announced it was shutting down VR support for its flagship Horizon Worlds platform.

Stock Performance:

Meta's share price fell more than 75% from its 2021 peak. Market cap dropped from approximately $1tn at $379 per share to $319bn, erasing over $680bn of investor capital. Free cash flow halved and margins compressed until Zuckerberg declared 2023 as the "year of efficiency", slashing headcount by 20% and later cutting metaverse/VR spending by another 30% in 2025.

Parallel to Google today:

The language ("large investments," "front-load investments," accept short-term pain for long-term leadership) mirrors exactly how Google is justifying its AI/TPU/data-center CapEx ramp today.

Below are extracts from Q4 2025 earnings transcripts from Google’s management remarks and Q&A.

Large investments:

“For the full year 2026, we expect CapEx to be in the range of $175 billion to $185 billion with investments ramping over the course of the year. We're investing in AI compute capacity to support Frontier model development by Google DeepMind, ongoing efforts to improve the user experience and drive higher advertiser ROI in Google Services, significant cloud customer demand as well as strategic investments in Other Bets.”

- Anat Ashkenazi.

Front-loading investments:

“To meet customer demand and capitalize on the growing opportunities ahead of us, our 2026 CapEx investments are anticipated to be in the range of $175 billion to $185 billion.”

- Sundar Pichai.

Accept short-term pain for long-term leadership:

“As we've discussed on previous calls, the significant increase in our investments in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L in the form of higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy.”

- Anat Ashkenazi.

From the above, it's clear how similar Meta's and Google's management are in their willingness to bet on what they believe is the future. The difference is we think the demand path for AI is more visible than it was for the metaverse. That said, Meta's painful experiment offers a warning of what could happen to Google if its AI market demand and monetization lag behind its massive and ambitious $175 to $185 bn bet. In later section, we will discuss metrics that we will monitor that would signal a lagging AI demand for investors.

Intel: The IDM 2.0 bet

In 2021, Intel CEO Gelsinger launched IDM 2.0, a vertical integration strategy promised to regain the process leadership from TSMC (TSM) and turn Intel into a major foundry for chip customers. He promised the massive U.S. and global fabs would deliver advanced nodes (18A, 14A) and revitalize the company via the AI tailwind.

There are many reasons why demand for Intel's fabs never materialized. We credit the single biggest reason to its failure due to its loss of technology leadership and manufacturing competitiveness to TSMC.

For major fabless customers such as Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO), they prioritized proven performance per watt (PPW), density, yield, and time to market. TSMC pulled ahead decisively on the 7nm/5nm nodes between 2018 and 2020 while Intel struggled with repeated delays on 10nm/7nm. By the time Intel reached 18A (1.8nm), TSMC’s N2 (2nm) was already ramping with superior ecosystem maturity and higher yields. Reports of Intel’s initial 18A yields around 60 to 65% made it too risky for external designs.

Because of the yield concerns, Nvidia paused testing and stuck with TSMC for Blackwell. Broadcom tested 18A and concluded it was not ready for large-scale production. Some hyperscalers, such as Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), showed interest but only committed modestly for diversification.

Intel Foundry’s total revenue was $17.8 billion in 2025, with 98% coming from internal wafers for its own CPUs/GPUs. Its external foundry revenue was only $307 million for the full year. Intel’s own 10-Q filings bluntly admit defeat in its external foundry business:

“We have been unsuccessful to date in securing any significant external foundry customers for any of our nodes and our prospects for securing a significant external foundry customer for Intel 14A are uncertain.”

Stock Performance:

Intel’s share price fell close to 75% peak-to-trough from $68 in 2021 to $17.70 in 2025. Market cap dropped from $278bn to $80bn. The company halted plants, took restructuring charges, and signaled it might pause or exit the foundry business if 14A demand failed to appear.

Parallel to Google today:

Google’s AI/TPU/data-center vertical buildout mirrors Intel’s vertical-integration bet. Both assumed that by building massive capacity ahead of the curve, demand would follow. Intel was ultimately right that the AI chip market would explode, it just didn’t explode for them. Google investors should watch carefully whether the company can win its AI battle and capture meaningful share as the AI market continues to grow.

What to Watch Out for at Google’s Upcoming Earnings

From the above two case studies, it’s obvious that while Google is pursuing an exciting opportunity, it is important that both: (1) the AI market continues to expand, and (2) Google doesn’t get its pie eaten by competition, in order to deliver returns for investors.

Below, we group five key metrics into two categories, AI market size and AI competition, and outline Google’s past performance and our expectations for the upcoming Q1 2026 earning results. These benchmarks will help us assess whether Google’s massive $175–$185 billion CapEx bet is justified.

1. AI Market Size: Is Demand Real and Accelerating? i.e., is the TAM big enough and growing fast enough to justify the $175–$185 billion 2026 CapEx?

Google’s management doesn’t provide their TAM number for the AI market. So we don’t know in their perspective, if the AI market is growing or not. Instead, they just repeatedly describe the AI market as a massive and accelerating opportunity that remains in its early stages.

Beyond the continued use of positive language such as “significant opportunity” and “extraordinary demand,” we expect in the upcoming Q1 2026 earnings that Google will deliver outperformance in the following metrics to show that AI market demand continues to grow as anticipated, thereby justifying its elevated CapEx spend:

  • Cloud Backlog: we think Cloud backlog is the best proxy for total AI market size. Backlog represents legally committed revenue from enterprises. An increase signals that more customers are locking in AI budgets. In Q4 2025, Google Cloud backlog was $240bn, +55% QoQ. For Q1 2026, we expect a similar 50%+ QoQ increase to signal that the overall AI market is continuing to expand rapidly.

  • Google Cloud Revenue: in Q4 2025, Google Cloud revenue printed $17.7bn, up 48% YoY, with an annual run rate of $70bn. For Q1 2026, we expect an inline print with consensus of 50%+ YoY to signal that the AI market is continuing to expand via revenue acceleration.

  • Gemini App MAU: this metric shows us that the AI market is expanding as usage moves from niche to mainstream. In Q4 2025, Gemini MAUs reached 750mm, with +100mm added in Q4. With the launch of features such as Personal Intelligence and Auto Browse, we think it will create new use cases and further drive MAU growth. Gemini App's disclosed user path was 450mm in July 2025, 650mm (+44%) in Q3 2025, 750mm (+15%) in Q4 2025. Our estimate for Q1 2026 would between 788mm (+5%) to 825mm (+10%).

2. AI Competition: Is Google Winning?

For consumer AI traffic, OpenAI still leads with 50% total traffic according to Similarweb March 2026 data. Gemini is trailing in second at 25% and Claude is in third with 10%. Gemini and Claude continue to gain ground as OpenAI’s consumer AI market share declined from 70% to 55% from August 2025 to March 2026.

For enterprise usage, Claude is currently leading with 40% market share vs. OpenAI at 25% vs. Gemini at 20%.

In terms of competition, we expect evidence of market share gains for Google vs. the other AI vendors given that Anthropic has recently tightened access and Google has just opened Gemini access to China (Hong Kong) users.

Key metrics:

  • Gemini Enterprise Paid Seats: rapid paid-seat growth is a clear market share win. It shows that Google can convert free trials into revenue and take market share from competitors in the competitive enterprise AI category. In Q4 2025, Gemini Enterprise sold 8mm+ paid seats in four months across 2,800 companies. For Q1 2026, our base case is 10.5mm cumulative paid seats, implying continued strong expansion but moderation from the initial launch burst.

  • Enterprise AI Adoption: high penetration amongst the most valuable customers (top SaaS and global enterprises) proves that Google is becoming the default AI platform. In Q4 2025, Google announced that more than 120,000 enterprises used Gemini, which includes 95% of the top 20 SaaS companies and 80% of the top 100 SaaS companies. These SaaS companies include global names such as Salesforce, Shopify, Airbus, Honeywell, etc. For Q1 2026, our prediction is 135,000 enterprises using Gemini, implying continued adoption growth, though at a more moderate pace than previous quarter.

Conclusion:

We are not yet convinced that Google is overspending on AI. The company enjoys more visible demand signals today than Meta or Intel did at similar points. That said, the painful lessons from Meta in 2022 and Intel in 2023 remain relevant. Both companies pursued classic “build it and they will come” strategies, pouring hundreds of billions into ambitious, front-loaded bets, backed by overconfident management with narratives about inevitable demand. In both cases, when adoption and monetization failed to keep pace, enormous amounts of shareholder value were destroyed.

Google's $175–$185 billion CapEx plan for 2026 faces the same test. The coming quarters will ultimately be decided by whether the AI market continues to expand rapidly and whether Google captures a meaningful and growing share of it.

On April 29th, we will be watching the exact metrics as outlined above: Cloud backlog growth, Cloud revenue acceleration, Gemini App MAU, Gemini Enterprise paid seats, and enterprise adoption trends. These data points will tell us whether Google is successfully executing on its bet or whether it risks repeating the costly mistakes of the past.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Flowers Foods: The Double-Digit Yield Is Hard To Ignore, Despite The Shaky Fundamentals
data.description:
Flowers Foods (FLO) has been on my radar for quite a while now, mainly because of its defensive nature and relative independence from the consumer confidence levels. I was not ready to pull the trigger, however, mainly because of the negative sentiment around the company in the past 12 months, with the stock price declining more than 60% over the period. Today, I believe that it is hard to ignore the firm based on the 11+% dividend yield, which appears to be safe at first sight, based on the company's cash flow generation. My aim is to provide you with a balanced view of the company and highlight some of the key pros and cons of investing in FLO's stock.

Financial performance

In the last quarter of fiscal 2025, FLO generated a revenue of $1.2 billion—11% more than they did in the year-ago period. While it looks impressive at first, I believe that the fact that this growth is mainly coming from acquisitions—4.7%—and the fact that the period contained an extra week—7.8%—makes this figure much less attractive. When it comes to growth, I generally tend to value organic, comparable growth much more, as that gives a much clearer picture of how the demand is shaping up for the products. If you look at the volume growth, it actually declined by 2.2% YoY, which was partially offset by the price/mix.

The question that I believe is important to answer here is whether this volume decline is temporary or structural. If we take a look at the chart, which shows volume trends for the past 5 quarters, it is difficult to say that it is temporary, as volumes were down each time.

On a positive note, I need to mention that the firm was able to achieve an increase in profitability, with operating margin up 0.3%, driven by a favourable price/mix and lower SD&A expenses. In my view, this margin expansion may not be long-lived due to the current geopolitical circumstances. Energy prices are elevated, and it is unclear when and how the Strait of Hormuz will be reopened, which is going to be a major driver of the oil prices in the coming months, which in turn also influences manufacturing and transportation costs.

Last, but not least, I would also like to raise my concerns about the capital structure of FLO. The firm's balance sheet is very debt-heavy, with more than $2 billion in total debt and only $12 million in cash. In my opinion, this is especially concerning now because of the uncertain interest rate environment. The next FOMC meeting in 5 days is going to be key for investors to watch to assess how the Fed may be acting going forward.

All in all, I do not find FLO's fundamentals very solid. I would like to see the sales dynamics fundamentally improving, and by that I mean that I want to see the growth being driven by volume and price/mix. It would be also good to get more clarity on the interest rate path to better understand the risks that come with the high debt. That said, I do believe, however, that the firm is currently attractively priced. Let us look at the valuation to see why I think so.

Dividends and valuation

As highlighted in the introduction, the main reason I am looking at FLO is its 11+% dividend yield; therefore, I believe a suitable way to assess the fair value of the stock is using a dividend discount model. But why is a dividend discount model actually applicable here? FLO has been a reliable dividend payer for more than two decades, and they even managed to increase these payments each year in the past 12 years. FLO currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.25, equivalent to an annual yield of 11.4%.

It is also important to highlight that the firm is generating enough free cash flow to cover the dividend payments.

When you look at the dividend safety grade, this may not be visible right away because most of the metrics indicate that FLO actually cannot afford the current payments. This picture is, however, distorted by the fact that FLO had a non-cash impairment of intangible assets, leading to negative earnings. But as it is a one-off non-cash item, it does not impact the safety of the dividend directly. Based on the cash flow metrics, the dividends are well covered.

Now that we have established that a dividend discount model is a valid approach, we have to define the two key input parameters for the model: required rate of return and dividend growth in the future. For the required rate of return, I will be using the company's current cost of equity, which is 7.34%.

For the dividend growth rate, I created two different scenarios, one that assumes a 2% growth in perpetuity and one that assumes 0% growth in perpetuity.

For the case, which assumes 2% growth, the resulting fair value is $20 per share, significantly higher than the current market price. In my view, 2% growth is not an aggressive assumption, as it is roughly equivalent to the long-term growth of the economy.

If you think that the 2% growth is too aggressive an assumption, let us take a look at the scenario where no growth is assumed at all. Even for this case, the resulting fair value is almost double the current share price. For these assumptions, the fair value per share is estimated to be $14.

It is not only the dividend discount models that show undervaluation. A set of traditional price multiples also indicates that FLO is currently undervalued compared to the sector median, as well as to its own historic metrics.

For these reasons, I find FLO attractive for income-focused investors.

Conclusions

From a fundamental point of view, I do not believe that FLO's business is particularly attractive. Although sales grew, it was mainly driven by the extra week compared to the prior year period and the acquisition of Simple Mills. The volume has already been trending down for several quarters now. The non-cash impairment charge also resulted in a net loss, and the high level of debt on the balance sheet also appears concerning.

Despite all this, I believe that FLO can still afford its dividends based on the cash flow generated, and based on my calculations, FLO is attractively valued, with a significant upside potential and a high margin of safety.

For these reasons, I believe that it is worth adding FLO to a dividend portfolio.
